snippets.title: Ridge and Lasso with scikit-learn
snippets.code:
from sklearn.linear_model import Ridge, Lasso
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.pipeline import make_pipeline
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score

ridge = make_pipeline(StandardScaler(), Ridge(alpha=1.0))
lasso = make_pipeline(StandardScaler(), Lasso(alpha=0.05, max_iter=10000))

for name, model in [("ridge", ridge), ("lasso", lasso)]:
    scores = cross_val_score(model, X, y, cv=5, scoring="neg_mean_squared_error")
    model.fit(X, y)
    coefs = model.named_steps[list(model.named_steps)[-1]].coef_
    nonzero = (coefs != 0).sum()
    print(f"{name}: CV MSE={-scores.mean():.3f}, nonzero coefs={nonzero}")
snippets.explanation: Always scale features before L1/L2 penalties so each column contributes fairly. `alpha` is λ; compare CV error and count non-zero Lasso coefficients.

snippets.title: Ridge closed-form solution in NumPy
snippets.code:
import numpy as np

# X: (n_samples, n_features), y: (n_samples,)
lam = 1.0
XtX = X.T @ X
Xty = X.T @ y
beta_ridge = np.linalg.solve(XtX + lam * np.eye(X.shape[1]), Xty)

beta_ols = np.linalg.lstsq(X, y, rcond=None)[0]
print("OLS norm:", np.linalg.norm(beta_ols))
print("Ridge norm:", np.linalg.norm(beta_ridge))
snippets.explanation: Adding λI to XᵀX stabilizes the solve. Ridge coefficients have smaller L2 norm than OLS when λ > 0.

snippets.title: Tune regularization strength with CV
snippets.code:
from sklearn.linear_model import RidgeCV, LassoCV
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.pipeline import make_pipeline

alphas = np.logspace(-3, 3, 50)

ridge_cv = make_pipeline(StandardScaler(), RidgeCV(alphas=alphas, cv=5))
lasso_cv = make_pipeline(StandardScaler(), LassoCV(alphas=alphas, cv=5, max_iter=10000))

ridge_cv.fit(X_train, y_train)
lasso_cv.fit(X_train, y_train)
print("best ridge alpha:", ridge_cv.named_steps["ridgecv"].alpha_)
print("best lasso alpha:", lasso_cv.named_steps["lassocv"].alpha_)
snippets.explanation: `RidgeCV` and `LassoCV` search a log-spaced grid of λ values and pick the one with lowest cross-validated error.
